summary: Errors when using is_instance_id_valid() and ways to debug objects/memory map

description.text:
### Tested versions

Godot 4.4.1-stable

### System information

Windows 10 - Godot Engine v4.4.1.stable.official.49a5bc7b6 - Forward+/Mobile/Compatibility - NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti

### Issue description

If you try "is_instance_id_valid()" on random ids, Godot will then spam this crash condition for absolutely no reasons:

 [godot/core/object/object.h](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/49a5bc7b616bd04689a2c89e89bda41f50241464/core/object/object.h#L1048)

What I used to do back in the Godot 3 days is something similar to what today would look like this:

```
for i in range(1000000):
	if is_instance_id_valid(i):
		var obj : Object = instance_from_id(i)
		if is_instance_valid(obj):
			print("%d: %d" % [i, obj.get_class()])
```
Then save it in a log, then compare multiple instance of those with a diff software to know if the memory used after an action is justified or not...

The output looks like this:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f681ec61-d100-49fb-a33d-3bfefa517157)

and the diff looks like this:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4b5e2c74-7eff-440e-8b1c-2bbf562ffe3c)

Sadly enough, Godot IDs are way too big now to use that kind of scam and the IDs are randomized too. I didn't find any issue on the subject which means I'm one of the last developers to actually check a memory map for actual memory leaks - **not the one you think are there, but the one you can observe**.

I'm working on a Tower Defense with strong Data-Oriented-Designed optimizations, and still at each wave, my game is generating hundreds of Godot Objects, which I can see in the Debugger.... but where is the tool to know what are those objects? Are those simple `InputEventKey` or some unmanaged `Texture`, I don't know and I have no way to know.

Why Godot has still no way to check the memory map of a game? Where is the function `get_used_instance_ids()` in the `@GlobalScope`? Why a validity check can be invalid enough to trigger an error? What is supposed to be the way of finding memory leaks with Godot?

### Steps to reproduce

execute:
```
for i in range(1000000):
	if is_instance_id_valid(i):
		var obj : Object = instance_from_id(i)
		if is_instance_valid(obj):
			print("%d: %d" % [i, obj.get_class()])
```

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[object_map_debugger.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21043424/object_map_debugger.zip)

Put this addon in any project, enable the addon, start the project, push CTRL + SHIFT + M, check for errors

summary: Can't inherit abstract class using inheritance tree selector when creating new script

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: master [efb40c1524e54f2cf52f213e248bd0a7b790975a]
- Reproducible in: 4.4.dev1
- Half-reproducible in: 4.3.dev1 (inherit button is not greyed out, but does nothing)

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (efb40c152) - Windows 11 (build 26100)

### Issue description

It's not possible to select the class you want to inherit/extend when using the inheritance tree selector in the Create Script dialog, if the class is abstract. When manually inputting the class name in the text field it does work however.

The inherit button's tooltip also wrongly states: "The selected class can't be **instantiated**", which is irrelevant in the context of inheriting it.

### Steps to reproduce

1. (Optional) Create a custom abstract class.
2. Start creation of a new script (e.g. by right-clicking a folder in FileSystem).
3. Click the Tree icon next to "Inherits:"
4. Attempt to inherit an abstract class (either your own, or a built-in one).

summary: Sub-window cannot go fullscreen, if `Window.FLAG_NO_FOCUS` is set to `true` in inspector

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: 4.4.1

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Ubuntu 20.04.6 LTS (Focal Fossa) on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 970 (nvidia; 535.183.01) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10700KF CPU @ 3.80GHz (16 threads)

### Issue description

A non-embedded sub window cannot go fullscreen, if you toggle `Unfocusable` in inspector to `true` and then instantiate it at some point

### Steps to reproduce

1. create a scene with a window as root node with mode set to fullscreen and flag `Unfocusable` to `true`
2. create a main scene with a button, that on click instantiates the scene above

_expected result_: sub-window should show in fullscreen  
_actual result_: sub-window stays at default size

**workaround**: leave `Unfocusable` set to `false`, and call `sub_window.set_flag(Window.FLAG_NO_FOCUS, true)` _after_ calling `sub_window.show()`


### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[fullscreen.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21056147/fullscreen.zip)

to test the workaround, go into `main.gd` and comment the signal connection to `_on_pressed` and uncomment the line below that one 
```gdscript
func _ready() -> void:
	self.pressed.connect(_on_pressed)
	#self.pressed.connect(_workaround)
```

summary: After a mouse exits a Control node and the containing window at the same time, `mouse_entered` signal is sometimes wrongly emitted immediately after `mouse_exited`

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: v4.4.1.stable, v4.3.stable, v4.3.dev6.official [89850d553]
- Not reproducible in: v4.0.stable, v4.2.2.stable, v4.3.dev3.official [36e943b6b], v4.3.dev4.official [df78c0636], v4.3.dev5.official [89f70e98d]

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6636) - 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-13900HX (32 threads)

### Issue description

After a mouse exits a Control node, `mouse_entered` signal is sometimes wrongly emitted immediately after `mouse_exited`. This leaves the client code mistakenly thinking that the node still has mouse hovering it.

Here's the script used in the MRP, attached to a screen-wide button:

```gdscript
extends Button

func _input(event: InputEvent) -> void:
	if event is InputEventMouseMotion:
		var mouse_pos: Vector2i = DisplayServer.mouse_get_position()
		var window_rect: Rect2i = Rect2i(get_tree().root.position, get_tree().root.size)
		print(Engine.get_physics_frames(), ": mouse_pos %s,  window_rect %s(has_mouse:%s)"
			% [mouse_pos, window_rect, window_rect.has_point(mouse_pos)])


func _on_mouse_entered() -> void:
	text = "entered"
	print(Engine.get_physics_frames(), ': entered')


func _on_mouse_exited() -> void:
	text = "exited"
	print(Engine.get_physics_frames(), ': exited')

```

... here's an example of the output log from a reproduction case:

```
227: entered
227: mouse_pos (2072, 1279),  window_rect [P: (1344, 726), S: (1152, 648)](has_mouse:true)
228: mouse_pos (2080, 1109),  window_rect [P: (1344, 726), S: (1152, 648)](has_mouse:true)
229: mouse_pos (2086, 831),  window_rect [P: (1344, 726), S: (1152, 648)](has_mouse:true)
230: mouse_pos (2086, 723),  window_rect [P: (1344, 726), S: (1152, 648)](has_mouse:false)
230: exited
230: entered
230: mouse_pos (2086, 631),  window_rect [P: (1344, 726), S: (1152, 648)](has_mouse:false)
```

... and here is a video capture:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0305babd-d120-411c-8dda-31653b71c02f

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open the MRP and run the main scene
2. Move the cursor across the game window, entering and exiting it
3. Observe that the window will occasionally get stuck on 'entered' state even after the cursor exits the window
4. Read the debug log to see that `mouse_exited` was actually emitted but `mouse_entered` was emitted immediately afterwards

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[mouse-exited-bug-mrp.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21040841/mouse-exited-bug-mrp.zip)

summary: Fix `SoftBody` - update rendering server once per frame.

description.text:
Also add selectable pinned point process mode.

## Discussion
This is some heavily work in progress (i.e. contains `DEV_ASSERT(0)` :grinning: ) stuff for fixing the `SoftBody` problems introduced in #106863 .

Hopefully one of the `SoftBody` interested guys will take this all over as I'm keen to avoid any responsibilities in this area.

## The difference between _USER_ callbacks and engine callbacks.

I.e. `PHYSICS_PROCESS` and `INTERNAL_PHYSICS_PROCESS`, `PROCESS` versus `PRE_FRAME_DRAW`.

Users are restricted to calling things at certain times, _engine developers_ on the other hand can use internal callbacks that can be assured to be _after_ or _before_ all user interactions. This is because a user might for example, move a bone, pin or whatever during a physics tick, or process, and we want to have rendering / physics react to that. (Users can use `process priority`, but the engine itself tends to separate user and internal into different phases).

This is commonly called "order of operations" and can cause a number of bugs, because moving one operation somewhere else can change the relative order, and create regressions elsewhere. This is notorious for causing problems, when a contributor is not aware of knock on effects.

Due to the chicken / egg effect, _sometimes there is no option but to add a frame of delay_.

Key things to note:
* There are some situations where it is better to update on the frame, some on the tick.
* Pinned points that are linked things that move on the tick make more sense to update on the tick (so the physics simulation is correct as possible). Pinned points that are linked to things that move on the frame (i.e. skeleton attachments) are more sensible to move on the frame, _AFTER_ the engine and user has had the opportunity to move the skeleton.
* The rendering server doesn't need to be updated on any occasion except prior to rendering. It doesn't care what state the soft body is in during the physics tick.
* In some situations, two frames will occur with no physics tick in between. In this case, without physics interpolation there is no need to update the rendering server because the data is not dirty. With physics interpolation, you still need an update because the interpolation fraction will be different.

## Notes
* Doesn't solve the existing threaded physics deadlock problem.

summary: ItemList Ctrl Click on Multi Selected Items Does Not Emit "item_clicked" Signal - Only Occurs When Deselecting an Item From the List of Selected Items

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: v4.5-beta2, v4.4.1-stable, v4.4.0, v4.3-stable, v4.2.2-stable, v4.1.2-stable.

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7602) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10870H CPU @ 2.20GHz (16 threads)

### Issue description

When using an ItemList with `select_mode` set to `multi`, and having selected a few items from the list of choices using `Shift + Left Click`, I find that deselecting a single item from that list (order does not matter) using `Ctrl + Left Click` does not cause the `item_clicked` signal to be emitted.
Even though `Ctrl + Left Click` on an unselected item does cause the `item_clicked` signal to be emitted, I would have imagined that doing the same thing to deselect the item would cause the same signal to be emitted, but it does not.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/de25167d-6608-485a-be24-4665d4b8be19)

Gif demonstrates what happens when trying to deselect items in a list of selected items and the prints to the console to visually confirm signal emission.

### Steps to reproduce

- Download, extract and run Minimal Reproduction Project.
- Run the *item_list_test_scene.tscn* scene in the editor.
- `Left Click` on any item in the list.
- `Shift + Left Click` on any other item in the list so that multiple items are now selected.
- Now press `Ctrl + Left Click` on an item in the list and notice how there is no output in the editor console.
- Pressing `Ctrl + Left Click` again on the same item, will show a new print statement in the editor console.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[itemlist_item_clicked_bug_mrp.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21029520/itemlist_item_clicked_bug_mrp.zip)

summary: Selecting the Forward Plus Renderer as the web renderer results in a black screen with no errors

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in 4.4.1 stable.

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated Qualcomm(R) Adreno(TM) X1-85 GPU (Qualcomm Incorporated; 31.0.96.0) - Snapdragon(R) X 12-core X1E80100 @ 3.40 GHz (12 threads)

### Issue description

If you select the "forward plus" renderer in project settings for the web renderer, the export works, but simply displays a black screen with no errors in the console.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3cd4928f-42c0-42fa-b5ff-1ccfada2999d)

It's expected that the renderer doesn't work, but I would expect that one or more of the following would occur:

 - I shouldn't be able to select the option
 - Selecting the option would display an error
 - There would be an error displayed in the export window (red text at the bottom)
 - There would be an error, similar to the cross-origin isolation/SharedArrayBuffer warning, visually during loading
 - There would be an error in the console when running the game

I should note, for me, I didn't manually select the option - it somehow was automatically set during an upgrade of a project from 4.2 to 4.4. However, I was unable to reproduce exactly how this happened. Regardless, since this is the case, I feel that making the option unselectable is not enough - one of the errors should also be displayed.

### Steps to reproduce

Set the setting in the image above and run the game using the "remote deploy" option at the top-right.

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproduced in Godot 4.5.beta2

Windows 11 - Vulkan 1.3.271 - Forward+ - Using Device #0: NVIDIA - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 Laptop GPU

### Issue description

After upgrading my project to 4.5 i noticed `Resource.duplicate(true)` was not working as before. I already expected this, but turns out none of the 3 option worked as i expected, and in particular `DEEP_DUPLICATE_ALL` (the one i need according to documentation) caused this assert because duplicating a `PackedScene` seems to attempt to create a duplicate of the Script, which is usually impossible because of the `PROPERTY_USAGE_NEVER_DUPLICATE` flag

### Steps to reproduce

- Create a new resource script like the following:
```
class_name MyResource extends Resource
@export var my_scene: PackedScene
```
- Create a scene script like the following:
```
class_name MyScene extends Node
```
- Create a new scene, assign the **MyScene** script and save it
- Create a new MyResource, assign the just created scene to the my_scene proeprty in the inspector and save it
- In the main scene of the project run the following code:
```
func _ready() -> void:
	preload("res://my_resource.tres").duplicate_deep(Resource.DEEP_DUPLICATE_ALL)
```

**Expected result**
- deep duplicate works, script is not duplicated

**Actual Result**
- The following error prompts
```
Parser Error: Class "MyScene" hides a global script class.
```

My understanding is the Script subresource of PackedScene is being duplicated and because of that is errors because of the repeated class_name

summary: TileSetScenesCollectionSource fails to instantiate new scenes when being modified during runtime

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in Godot Engine v4.4.1.stable.official.49a5bc7b6, Godot Engine v4.3.stable.official.77dcf97d8, 

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1060 with Max-Q Design (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7640) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-8750H CPU @ 2.20GHz (12 threads)

### Issue description

When using a TileMapLayer or TileMap node with a Scene Collection as a Tileset, the inbuilt `.set_cell()` fails to instantiate any scenes when called specifically during runtime (for example when called inside `func _physics_process()` or `func _input()`). 

Note:
- This problem does not extend to any scene tiles that have been placed in the editor, meaning the tilemap correctly instantiates in scene tiles at `_ready()`
- the inbuilt functions `.get_used_cells()` and `.get_used_cells_by_id()` still correctly list any tiles that were spawned in during runtime. meaning that the issue is with the scene instantiation and not with modifying a tilemap during runtime
- the tilemap correctly frees any pre-existing scene tiles, either through the `.erase_cell()` function or by using `.set_cell()` to overwrite a pre-existing scene tile. 

### Steps to reproduce

Both TileMapLayer and Tilemap use a Scene Collection Tileset consisting of a single basic scene.
```gdscript
func _input(event: InputEvent) -> void:
     if event is InputEventKey:
          $TileMapLayer.set_cell(Vector2i(0, 0), 0, Vector2i(0,0))
          $TileMap.set_cell(Vector2i(0, 0), 0, Vector2i(0,0))
```

summary: SoftBody3D uses outdated skeleton modifications

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in 4.5.beta2, 4.5.beta1
Not reproducible in 4.5.dev5 and earlier

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta2 - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4060 Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7680) - 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13500HX (20 threads) - 15.72 GiB memory

### Issue description

I have a SoftBody3D with its vertices pinned to the bones of a Skeleton3D that is animated with SkeletonModifier3D. After the changes introduced in #106863, SoftBody lags behind the Skeleton when it is animated. 

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ff0ee2c1-1445-4547-b915-0ef91dbe86ce)

Occasionally, when new animation is played by the AnimationPlayer, SoftBody jumps to the skeleton pose set by the AnimationPlayer instead of the override that should be enforced by the SkeletonModifier, causing a transient but noticeable visual glitch. 

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ed25fb1b-1fea-4366-8c22-c29585fa2a6a)

This issue appears regardless of whether `physics_interpolation` is enabled or not and it seems to be a race condition caused by the transition from `frame_pre_draw` signal to `NOTIFICATION_INTERNAL_PROCESS` and `NOTIFICATION_INTERNAL_PHYSICS_PROCESS`, which results in SoftBody being updated before `_process_modification()` is resolved.

### Steps to reproduce

Run the MRP. The Skeleton will be animated by SkeletonModifier to play the looped "Flap" animation. Notice the gap between the SoftBody and wing bones. You can pause with F9 to examine it up close.
Tap _Space_ several times until a visual glitch appears: for a moment, SoftBody's mesh will jump towards the center, then back. 

Pressing _Space_ resets the AnimationPlayer, which plays an animation with folded wings. It is expected that this animation shouldn't be seen as it is normally overriden by _process_modificaton(), but because of the issue this doesn't always happen.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[softbody-skeletonmodifier.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21021899/softbody-skeletonmodifier.zip)

summary: Button 'pressed' signal called twice delayed on double-click on non-focused window

description.text:
### Tested versions

Godot v4.4.1.stable

### System information

macOS Ventura (13.7.2) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - Intel(R) Iris(TM) Plus Graphics OpenGL Engine - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-1068NG7 CPU @ 2.30GHz (8 threads)

### Issue description

In the attached project, you will find a simple UI with a single button that must be pressed to trigger the unexpected/inconsistent behaviour. Start the project and switch away from the window to click the button while window is unfocused. Repeat with different timings of repeated clicks, even doing a double click.

You can use the "run multiple instances" in Debug menu, to conveniently switch back and forth between two windows for this.

See attached video, where I perform those steps:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/123818b3-5bea-4b8c-8415-437044250433

---

Observed behaviour (on window with focus):
1. **one single click**: signal emitted once (ok)
2. **two single clicks (slow)**: signal twice (ok)
3. **double click (slow)**: signal emitted twice (ok)
4. **double click (fast)**: signal emitted twice (ok)

Observed behaviour (on window without focus):
1. **one single click**: window becomes focused; no signal emitted (ok)
2. **two single clicks (slow)**: window becomes focused; signal emitted once (ok)
3. **double click (slow)**: window becomes focused; signal emitted twice (**unexpected**)
4. **double click (fast)**: window becomes focused; signal emitted twice (**unexpected**)

Consuming "the first click" fully to focus a window is not uncommon on macOS. But the double-click behaviour on the non-focused window feels awkward, especially since the "two clicks" are delayed until the double-click gesture is finished...?

Suggested behaviour for emitting click-events when window is not focused:

- **Option A**: don't consume a click to focus the window
- **Option B**: consume a click to focus the window, deliver the second click of the double click as a regular click

Either option would be fine for me, with Option B feeling "more natural" on macOS maybe.



### Steps to reproduce

- connect to button's pressed signal
- unfocus window
- double-click button
- observe, that the button is clicked once or twice, depending on delay of clicks

summary: Unable to close previous BBCode tags if new ones are opened - RichTextLabel

description.text:
### Tested versions


Found and reproducible in:
- v4.4.1.stable.mono.official [49a5bc7b6]
- v4.4.stable.official [4c311cbee]
- v4.3.stable.mono.official [77dcf97d8]

not tested on earlier versions

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Windows 10.0.26100 - Vulkan (Forward+)

### Issue description

### Issue
When parsing BBCode, I am unable to close a previously opened tag as long as there is a new and different one opened.

For example, when setting text as "`Hello [b]how are you [i]heheh [/b]I am fine[/i] and you?`", the "[/b]" tag is not parsed and it is fully printed on text, instead of closing the previous "[b]" one. 
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6fbbf8d8-9df0-4d38-a5fc-4b4fdeb6fa46)

It seems that the "[i]" becomes the new priority of the parsing and as long as it's opened, the [b] can't be closed. 
In fact, if I add [/b] _after_ the [i] closes, then it's finally taken into account and closed. "`Hello [b]how are you [i]heheh [/b]I am fine[/i][/b] and you?`"
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/890b1f69-d7f3-4edb-abd5-7a90bcc89328)

The **workaround** is to wrap the previous tag ("[b]" in this case), closing and reopening it between the "new" tag ("[i]" in this case), whenever we know we want to close it _before_ the new one closes/resolves, like this: "`Hello [b]how are you [/b][i][b]heheh [/b]I am fine[/i] and you?`"
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7cbe4d13-2885-4910-8780-8a6f2a15ba8b)

---

### Expected Result
I expect to be able to close an already opened tag anywhere later in the text, even if new/different tags were opened.

For example, the text "`Hello [b]how are you [i]heheh [/b]I am fine[/i] and you?`" should produce the following result:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7cbe4d13-2885-4910-8780-8a6f2a15ba8b)

This is useful for stacking multiple tags on top of each other and have a better user experience in closing them, without having to manually track the "current parsing priority" and wrap things around (which also becomes heavy work if there are many tags in the text). 

### Steps to reproduce

Steps to reproduce:
- Create a RichTextLabel Node
- Enable BBCode
- Add the following text: "`Hello [b]how are you [i]heheh [/b]I am fine[/i] and you?`"
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9d05e8fa-fce5-4571-b738-7058054684b6)

N/A

summary: Use create script dialog for script-type resources

description.text:
While the `FileSystemDock` provides a "Create New -> Script" option, it has been brought to my attention that some of our users are attempting to use the "Create New -> Resource" and then selecting a script type. While this works, it creates an empty script, which is less than desirable because the user isn't taking advantage of choosing a parent type or utilizing any of the templates a language may expose.

This change builds on what we already do if a user selects a shader-based resource type. If the chosen type extends `Script`, the user is redirected to the `CreateScriptDialog` instead. This change enforces the same navigation flow as if the user had selected "Create New -> Script," and it leverages the language-driven script templates and parent type selection process that one would expect when creating script-based resources.

summary: Add missing layout_mode into the control.xml

description.text:
Hi, this is my first contribution!

I noticed that the 'layout_mode' property in the Control node shows up in the Inspector but wasn't documented anywhere in the class reference. I found issue godot-docs#11071 mentioning the same problem, so I tried to help by adding it.

In this PR, I added:

- The 'LayoutMode' enum with its two values.
- A note that it's only editable from the Inspector and not accessible from scripts (based on how it's set up in the engine).

I'm not sure if I formatted everything perfectly since I'm still learning how the docs system works, so feedback is very welcome.

Thank you!

summary: Blender's default scene light is too bright when imported from a .blend file

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: v4.4.1.stable.arch_linux

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable unknown - Arch Linux #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Fri, 27 Jun 2025 16:35:07 +0000 on Wayland - Wayland display driver, Single-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 6800 XT (RADV NAVI21) - AMD Ryzen 9 5900X 12-Core Processor (24 threads)

### Issue description

Importing `.blend` files from Blender seem to result in way too bright lights, likely due to #73624.

I'm making a separate issue, because #73624 is about glTF files, and there's a lack of clarity regarding the spec, which understandably blocks that one. But even though Godot's .blend import works by using Blender to export into glTF and then importing that glTF (afaik), this issue could be fixed for Blender files specifically, as there's a clear target: match whatever Eevee and Cycles output, at least when the tonemapper and exposure values are matched and the project is using physical light units (and thus they ought to be aiming for the same thing as Blender's renderers).

I'm hoping that fixing this would be as easy as using one of the alternate Lighting Modes in the Blender glTF export process in Godot's .blend import process, but if that's not it, I hope there's other sane ways to fix this. Would smooth out authoring levels with Blender a lot.

I'm interested in contributing a fix for this and will be debugging this further, but thought I'd post an issue before a PR, in case I'm missing something.

| Cycles | Eevee | Godot |
|--|--|--|
| ![The test scene rendered with Blender Cycles](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4397e30d-34f0-4748-9fa8-bd42ebbbaedc) | ![The test scene rendered with Blender EEVEE](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c4fea115-ae19-4685-b3a9-50a29c94cfeb) | ![The test scene rendered with Godot Forward+, see more settings in the "Steps to reproduce" section](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3980be7c-e246-446c-876d-5d6439e67408) |

### Steps to reproduce

My methodology with the MRP was:
- Take the default Blender cube scene and save it with minimal changes (ended up changing the render resolution to match Godot's default), and import that into Godot for comparison.
- On the Godot side:
  - Toggle Physical Light Units to be enabled.
  - Add a WorldEnvironment which uses AgX as the tonemapper (same one Blender uses by default, though their parameters differ — Blender has "Exposure" 0.0 and "Gamma" 1.0, where Godot has "Exposure" 1.0. I'm assuming these represent the same values in the end.)
  - Also in the WorldEnvironment, use the same `#404040` background color as Blender, at 1.0 nits Intensity (because that seems to result in the background actually being that color, much like Blender's default "Strength" 1.0).
- Render the .blend with Cycles, EEVEE, and take a screenshot of the running Godot project.

I hope I didn't forget to document any steps, but I've included the final MRP (which includes the .blend) for others to test.

If there's any additional steps which are needed to match Godot's PBR parameters to Blender's defaults, I'd love to hear of them and account for them here. For what it's worth, not doing any of the steps on Godot's side also resulted in similarly `#FFFFFF` results.

[blender-light-brightness-mrp.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21006733/blender-light-brightness-mrp.zip)

summary: Allow moving nodes when they have different parents in SceneTreeDock

description.text:
Previously SceneTreeDock didn't allow to move selected nodes up and down (Ctrl + Up/Down by default) when they have different parents. The code suggests that this was intentional, but I don't like it. I don't see any issues with moving nodes with different parents.
So to implement this, I just removed the limitation that was there before. Also, to get selected nodes it uses now `get_full_selected_node_list)` instead of `get_top_selected_node_list()`, so you can move a node and its children at the same time. This is the only change I'm not sure about. But right now I would prefer this approach over the previous one.

summary: SoftBody3D editor gizmo does not highlight pinned points correctly

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in 4.5-beta1 and earlier versions

### System information

Windows 11

### Issue description

When editing a SoftBody3D, the editor gizmo shows handles at each mesh vertex.  Clicking on one of these handles is supposed to pin the point, and then show it highlighted in blue.  However, depending on the mesh it usually does not highlight the pinned vertices correctly.

The problem appears to be that the editor gizmo draws one handle per vertex in the rendered mesh, and not one handle per physics vertex.  In most meshes, there are many render vertices at the same coordinates (just with different normals and UV coordinates from one another).

For instance, consider the following mesh:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/00517f05-2269-42f4-bcc5-3206821f0d6a)

This `BoxMesh` has 24 vertices: there are 3 vertices at each corner of the cube, since each face has a different normal.  However, from a physics perspective the soft body only has 8 vertices that get simulated.

The editor is showing 24 separate handles here, and is displaying 3 separate handles on top of each other at each corner.  When you click on a handle it attempts to render it in blue, but in practice one of the non-highlighted handles usually renders on top of it, so you can't tell that it is highlighted.

The soft body gizmo seems like it should be fixed to only display 8 handles in this case, so it can correctly display which handles should be highlighted.  (In practice there are only 8 logical vertices that can be pinned; each physics vertex will be pinned if any one of the associated render vertices are pinned.)

### Steps to reproduce

- Add a SoftBody3D to a scene
- Attach a BoxMesh to it
- Try clicking on some of the points to highlight it

In practice this will happen with many other mesh types as well.  Imported ArrayMesh objects often have this problem.
The SphereMesh also has this problem at the ends of the sphere and along a seem down one segment of the sphere
The primitive PlaneMesh type is one of the few mesh types that does not have this problem, because it has a simple 1:1 mapping between render vertices and physics vertices.

summary: "Copy Value" in Debugger > Stack Trace > Stack Variables does not copy anything

description.text:
### Tested versions

I am running the Standard Linux versions
- Reproducible in: 
   - **v4.5.beta1.official [46c495ca2]** (Godot_v4.5-beta1_linux.x86_64)
   - **v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6]** (Godot_v4.4.1-stable_linux.x86_64)
   - **v4.0.stable.official [92bee43ad]** (Godot_v4.0-stable_linux.x86_64)
- Not Reproducible in:
   - Didn't check the v3.x yet. May or may not be a regression.

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Linux Mint 22 (Wilma) on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Mobile) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 (nvidia; 550.144.03) - 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13400F (16 threads)

### Issue description

While in debug mode, the Stack Trace tab has a right click context menu for each stack variable with options like -
```
- Copy Value (Ctrl + C)
- Paste Value (Ctrl + V)
- Copy Property Path (Ctrl + Shift + C)
```
`Copy Property Path` works fine, however `Copy Value` fails to put anything in the clipboard.
This happens for nested values too (Arrays and Dictionaries)

### Steps to reproduce

You can run the attached MRP or simply create a new project with 1 scene.

1. In the `_ready()` method of your main scene add the following code -
```gdscript
func _ready() -> void:
	var a = "Some string"
	var b = []
	
	var b1 = {
		"someProp": 123,
		"anotherProp": "678"
	}
	
	b.append(b1)
	
	print("Add Breakpoint here!")
	print("Inspect a,b and values in b1. Debugger menu > Stack Track Tab > Stack Variables")
	print("Both Locals and Members variables support right click -> Copy property path")
	print("BUT right click -> Copy value does not work")
```

2. Add a breakpoint on the statement `print("Add Breakpoint here!")`
3. Run the project and when execution halts at the breakpoint, attempt to `Right click` > `Copy Value` or  `Ctrl + C` after selecting any of `a`,`b`, `b1.someProp` and `b[0].anotherProp` from the Debugger > Stack Trace > Stack Variables list
   a. **Expected Outcome**: The values of these variables should be present in the clipboard
   b. **Actual Outcome**: Nothing is present in the clipboard
4. Run the project and when execution halts at the breakpoint, attempt to `Right click` > `Copy Property Path` or  `Ctrl + Shift + C` after selecting any of `a`,`b`, `b1.someProp` and `b[0].anotherProp` from the Debugger > Stack Trace > Stack Variables list
   a. **Expected Outcome**: The path of the variable should be copied
   b. **Actual Outcome**: The path of the variable is indeed present in the clipboard

summary: Top down CharacterBody2D doesn't work with AnimatableBody2D for moving platforms

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in Godot 4.4

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Pop!_OS 22.04 LTS on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NV167 - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-9300HF CPU @ 2.40GHz (8 threads)

### Issue description

CharacterBody2D doesn't include physics for top down moving/rotating platforms.

I expect that if I set "Motion Mode" to "Floating" and match the CharacterBody2D "Floor Layers" with AnimatableBody2D "Collision > Layer" then the AnimatableBody2D is treated as a top down moving platform, but it seems to ignore the setting.

Implementing it with workarounds requires extra nodes, signals, and scripting logic, but still has buggy behaviour. And that's only me trying moving platforms (position), which has very basic math. Rotating will require calculating offset of player from platform, then detecting and applying platform rotation as player movement, because the player needs to change position in a circle based on changing rotation of platform.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Create **following scene tree**:

   ```
   - Node2D
       - CharacterBody2D
           - CollisionShape2D
       - AnimatableBody2D
           - CollisionShape2D
       - AnimationPlayer
   ```

2. Add collision shapes. 

   I chose RectangleShape2D.

   I also increased size of AnimatableBody2D > CollisionShape2D.

3. **Enable "Visible Collision Shapes"** in "Debug" menu (top left).

   This setting shows collisions well when running the game, and is required to see anything without adding sprites.

4. On CharacterBody2D, set **"Motion Mode" to "Floating"**.

   This setting is meant for top down movement, and changes some physics interactions and settings. It **should also affect moving platform physics, but seems to disable it**.

5. On AnimatableBody2D, set **"Collisions > Layer" to only layer 2**. Disable all other layers.

   With this setting, CharacterBody2D should not collide with side of AnimatableBody2D, so that it can "stand on top" of it by overlapping (in top down, overlapping means standing on top).

   Also, because CharacterBody2D has layer 2 in "Moving Platform > Floor Layers", I **expect it to think AnimatableBody2D with collision layer 2 is "floor"** in _top down_, so when it moves, then CharacterBody2D should move with the "floor platform".

6. Create **animation to move AnimatableBody2D**:

   1. In Animation bottom panel, create animation with "Animation > New".

   2. In Animation bottom panel, enable **"Autoplay on Load"** (button near top right of panel).

   3. In Animation bottom panel, set **Animation Looping to Ping Pong** by clicking the loop button twice (below the pin icon in top right of panel). The button should become an arrow pointing left and right to two standing lines.

      With this setting, only one direction needs to be set, and Godot will automatically animate it in the opposite direction.

   4. In Animation bottom panel, "Add Track > Property Track > AnimatableBody2D > position".

   5. In AnimatableBody2D, set "Transform > Position" to prevent overlap with CharacterBody2D and set keyframe (press key icon).

   6. In Animation bottom panel, go to last frame.

   7. In AnimatableBody2D, change "Transform > Position" and set keyframe (press key icon).

7. Add **top down movement script** to CharacterBody2D (given below).

8. Run project and move CharacterBody2D with arrow keys to touch AnimatableBody2D.

   The CharacterBody2D should be moved with the AnimatableBody2D, but it is not affected at all.

#### Top down movement script

```gdscript
extends CharacterBody2D


const SPEED = 300.0


func _physics_process(_delta: float) -> void:
	var direction_x := Input.get_axis("ui_left", "ui_right")
	if direction_x:
		velocity.x = direction_x * SPEED
	else:
		velocity.x = move_toward(velocity.x, 0, SPEED)

	var direction_y := Input.get_axis("ui_up", "ui_down")
	if direction_y:
		velocity.y = direction_y * SPEED
	else:
		velocity.y = move_toward(velocity.y, 0, SPEED)

	move_and_slide()
```

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/61af245f-df73-431b-b364-beba389045d4

[top-down-moving-platform.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20978112/top-down-moving-platform.zip)

Player character shows up as rectangle in top left. Move right and down towards moving platform.
